description: Monday 15 September 2025 | Finance and Expenditure Committee Financial Markets (Conduct of Institutions) Amendment (Duty to Provide Financial Services) Amendment Bill Mindful Money Barry Coates, Founder and Co-CEO RNZSPCA Dr Arnja Dale, Chief Scientific Officer (Science, Education & SPCA Certified) Marie McAninch, Scientific Officer – Farmed Animals Lance Wiggs Jen Olsen Gray Eatwell
descriptionSummary: Financial Markets (Conduct of Institutions) Amendment (Duty to Provide Financial Services) Amendment Bill. Mindful Money. Barry Coates, Founder and Co-CEO

Barry, can you hear me all right? We can see you on screen. Just need your video and audio on.

We're off to a cracking start here on a Monday morning at the races. Andy foster's here in the shadows. Uh, welcome, Andy. Um, Barry, can you hear me at all, or can you miss Not in, not in the shadows at all. I am trying to get the it to work though, which has been bit challenging.

This sounds like you're not the only one. Oh, Barry. Yep. Your audio's off. Good. Can you hear me now? Yeah, we can. Yeah, just are you able to turn video on or? Um, and we've got you for about another nine minutes, so perhaps just, um, get into your presentation and if you wanna leave some time for questions at the end.

Sure. Great. So, um, thank you committee for hearing this evidence. Uh, uh, my name's Barry Cos. I'm, I'm co CEO founder and co CEO of a charity called Mindful Money that, uh, operates within the final finance sector and offers transparency to investors about what kind of, uh, companies they invest in, in their KiwiSaver or their retail funds.

So we're closely involved in, uh, uh, in both investment and, and banking issues, uh, but somewhat from an outsider's perspective, rather than being one of the commercial entities. So that gives us an interesting perspective on the industry. Um, excuse me. Uh, we don't support this bill, um, primarily because we think that the, um, identification of ESG with discriminatory policy on the basis of, of, uh, uh, some kind of, uh, supposedly, uh, uh, altruistic view misses the real point of, of ESG, which is

that it is primarily a risk management tool, and it's applied not only widely by the investment and banking sector in New Zealand, but internationally, um, including in the UN principles of responsible investment where there's over 120 trillion US dollars, uh, under management from institutions that adhere or, or have signed up to, um, principles for, for ESG risk management.

So that seems to us to, to be kind of squarely in the mainstream of financial practice and increasingly managing risks such as climate risks, uh, uh, material to the business and, uh, can, can be demonstrated through evidence of, uh, of past practice and, and past returns for those who, who are adopting ESG policies.

So, so this is not a, uh, I think not a, a case of, of, uh, sort of woke behavior. It's a, it's a case of, of good financial management being, being applied. Um, secondly, I I don't think it's good for, for the users in the industry to have, uh, have, uh, some applications of the SG policy being out ruled and, and not others.

I think it's, uh, uh, it's an important tool for the industry as a whole, and it's important for users to be able to have the consistency of policy across, uh, across all uses. I think it would be an extremely difficult policy to be able to, to, uh, differentiate, uh, between when, when it applies or, or not.

Uh, thirdly, I think it sets, uh, a kind of dangerous precedent in terms of, of, uh, a judging that certain financial policies, uh, don't meet certain, uh, criteria. Uh, I think that's fine when consumers make those choices voluntarily. I think it's a little bit different when regulators step in and somewhat arbitrarily decide what's permissible and what isn't.

Uh, and, and finally on, uh, uh, uh, I think it represents a, uh, uh, an intrusion of legislation into financial markets in a way that that isn't really supported by the evidence. So, so that's our submission, uh, and I'm very happy to take questions.

Thanks, Bruce. Uh, first question is from, uh, Ingrid Leary. Thanks, uh, lovely to see you. I'm wondering, given that normally we would expect, um, parties that sit on the right to want free flowing markets and minimize market intervention, what you make of this incursion, as you've described it, a uh, an unwarranted intrusion into the financial sector, and you've called it an overreach.

Can you speak to what the possible agenda behind this might be, given that it seems out of whack with the political side that is, uh, including this into the legislative program? Thank you. Uh, thank you, OOD. I, I can understand that, uh, there is some concern about potential, uh, uh, kind of differences of opinion on, on the application of some principles in the finance sector, like, like the ESG principles.

However, I think, you know, they, they are squarely in the, in the wheelhouse of good, good, uh, financial practice. And I think, um, the legislative overreach comes in terms of, of it being unclear as to what the problem is that potential legislation is trying to solve here.

And I think if we, uh, if we start, uh, um, uh, how should I say, uh, um, interventions without strong evidential support of a problem with financial markets, then we can get into a really difficult situation. And, uh, so that's where, where, uh, the basis of our concern about s of overreach.

Okay. Thank you. I've got a question from Andy Foster. Yeah, look, thanks Barry. well, look, we are gonna get out of that business by 2030 in which in fact we're gonna do progressively, um, by 2030. Isn't that in itself the thing that will, um, effectively close those businesses down? It's not an ESG thing. Um, what stage in the balance does, uh, does the financial sector overreach itself in making those judgements?

Um, Andy, I think that's a good question. Thank you for it. Uh, and I think they have done their research around trends in the retailing of, um, oil, oil and gas products, retailing of petrol and diesel.

I, I think they, uh, um, they have justifiable concerns that this is an industry where there is a huge amount of capital expenditure and infrastructure, which is very hard to amortize as volumes decline and volumes will decline. And when that decline comes, it may well be rapid, uh, and prices may, may fall, as we saw during the COVID era where oil prices actually went negative. I think there are serious risks in the downstream retailing sector for oil and gas. I think what, what funds are, what their finance sector is saying that banks are saying is that they don't want to be holding that risk.

Now, the question is, so, so what if nobody wants to hold that risk, then, then indeed, as you say, there becomes a public policy problem. But I don't think we're anywhere near there yet. But if we were, if banks all ran down the same, the same pathway as one or two have chosen to do, then we would be in that position, wouldn't we?

And we've got a vehicle fleet of four and a half odd million vehicles, most of which would then be completely useless and ability to get around and get around freight and all those sort of things. So, yeah. Yeah, yeah, yeah. The problem is that they may have to pay a bit more for it. And that's, uh, that's the nature of the changes, and it's the nature of the risks associated with their business. When the risk is higher, the price goes up. Thanks. Um, animal welfare is also a legitimate financial risk.

So that, um, sort of invites confusion. I suppose, if, if financial organizations are allowed to make decisions on, on financial risk, but not ESG, um, then that doesn't really make sense to us. Um, because if we think of things like investing in certain farming practices like building, um, sheds for colony cages in the industry, um, building sheds for flowering crates, uh, in the pork industry, investing in things like new live export ships, um, you know, all of these things are uncertain.

Um, these are infrastructure investments which cost millions. They have lifespans of, you know, 20, 30 years for a new shed, for example. Um, and it is a responsible decision to start thinking about whether things like that are going to be acceptable in 30 years time. Um, so that is a, uh, to us that's more of a financial risk management decision, and it is a, a woke decision or an ESG decision, right?

They're not really separable. Um, and so for that reason, the bill as drafted doesn't really make a lot of sense to us. The secondary reason, um, that I wanted to talk to, uh, talk about today, um, basically was about maybe some unintended consequences.

So, uh, organizations like ours, um, like the environment NGOs that you might be hearing from, obviously we're going to, um, keep doing the work that we do and keep pushing for changes, right? We're not going away, and we have a few different pathways to push for changes over time. Um, and one of them is, for example, to call for changes to law.

Um, but also we, we work with businesses, we work with farmers, we work with financial institutions, um, and we try and see change by, by applying consumer pressure, for example. Um, and we've just seen in the past couple of months that, uh, Sweden, for example, has announced that they've effectively achieved a ban on all pages and farming, and that's not through the law.

That was only through consumer pressure. Um, and, you know, probably some, uh, some investment into transitions and things like that, right? Uh, from US perspective, that's maybe a more gentle or easier way to achieve that kind of change over time.

Um, you know, calling for a total ban on things like that can be, um, disruptive, uh, or, um, create a lot of pressure on industries. Um, so basically just wanted to highlight that if the government starts removing these tools, um, of the free market, for example, to, to help with transitions over time or to provide more favorable investment, um, to certain types of businesses, um, then that basically leaves us pushing more towards things like bans, total bans and colony cages or fair and creates, um, which might create more sudden change.

Um, so those were the two major points really I wanted to make. Um, obviously happy to answer any questions, um, and the rest of the submission as well. Um, yeah, as, as summarized, SPCA recommends that the bill is either withdrawn or overhauled, um, to permit ESG based due diligence.

Uh, and we're, we're happy to take questions. Thanks, Marie. Um, just a quick one for me.
